title: Beech candle box with pull-out lid
subtitle: A small rectangular box with a sliding panel lid in beech
excerpt: A small rectangular box in beech with a pull-out sliding lid. The lid runs in a pair of saw-cut grooves in the box sides. A traditional mantelpiece box for candles, matches, or small objects. An entry-level project that introduces marking-out, simple carcass construction, and sliding-groove joinery.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for small box construction and sliding-lid grooves. Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the candle box form and domestic use.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable moisture content, dimensionally stable for precise box-making.
A stepped groove cut along an edge or face to accept a panel, lid, or another board. In this box, the rebate is the groove that the lid slides in.
The slot left by a saw blade. Two parallel kerfs form the rebate groove for the pull-out lid.

content.text: Cutting tools are sharp. Work with the blade travelling away from your body and your free hand behind the cutting edge at all times. Strop the tool before starting and whenever the edge feels rough.
A candle box is a small open-fronted box, roughly 30 cm long, 10 cm wide, and 8 cm deep, with a thin panel lid that slides in a
rebate
groove cut in the inside face of each long side. The
seasoned
beech must be dry; any residual movement will bind the sliding lid.
Components

Two long sides: 30 cm x 8 cm x 9 mm. Two short ends: 10 cm x 8 cm x 9 mm. One base: 30 cm x 10 cm x 6 mm. One lid panel: 30 cm x 10 cm x 6 mm, with a small pull knob or chamfered edge at one end for gripping. All pieces in beech.
Cutting the lid grooves

The lid groove runs along the inside face of each long side, 8 mm from the top edge and 6 mm wide. To cut the groove: set the marking gauge to 8 mm and scribe the near edge of the groove. Reset to 14 mm and scribe the far edge. Saw along each
kerf
line to 4 mm depth with the tenon saw. Remove the waste between the two saw cuts with the mortise chisel, paring the groove flat.
Box carcass assembly

Glue the two end pieces between the long sides, keeping all joints square. Clamp with the sash cramp across the long axis and check square by measuring the diagonals. Let the glue set fully before adding the base. Glue the base on from underneath, flush with the bottom edges of the sides.
Fitting the lid

Plane or sand the lid panel until it slides smoothly in the grooves with no binding or wobble. The lid should move with light finger pressure and stop when pushed to the closed position. Chamfer the pull end of the lid panel at 45 degrees with the chisel to give a grip; this also prevents the lid from being pushed too far in and jamming.
Finishing

Sand the outside surfaces with 400-grit paper, working along the grain. Apply two coats of danish oil with a cloth. Do not finish the inside of the lid grooves or the lid edges; the oil swells the fibres slightly and can cause binding.
Variations
Dovetail corners: replace the glued butt corners with through-dovetails for a stronger and more visible joint.
Recipe card box: widen the box to 15 cm and increase the depth to 12 cm for holding index cards.

rows.symptom: Lid binds in the groove and will not slide smoothly
rows.cause: Lid panel is fractionally too wide, or finish has been applied to the groove
rows.fix: Remove the lid and pare a thin shaving from each long edge with the chisel. Test after each pass. Do not apply finish to the groove walls.

rows.symptom: Box carcass is not square after clamping
rows.cause: Sash cramp not aligned parallel to the long sides during clamping
rows.fix: Realign the cramp. If the glue is still wet, square the box by hand before it sets. Measure the diagonals: they must be equal.
